narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—about a 51% chance of not passing. Picture your application as a story the model read closely: your LSAT score of 31 stood out as the biggest chapter pushing toward that risk, like the opening line that set a challenging tone right away. Then came your Year 1 decile of 1, the second strongest signal, showing early struggles in law school that echoed those initial hurdles. Your Year 3 decile of 1 built on that, reinforcing a pattern of being at the lower end of your class, making the model worry about building momentum. Even with your upper-middle family income, which might suggest some support, it surprisingly nudged the outlook toward failure in the model's eyes, perhaps because it didn't offset the academic trends as strongly as hoped. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 added another layer, whispering doubts about foundational readiness, while your Native American background and male gender played supporting roles in tipping the scales. Going full-time sealed the narrative's arc, painting a picture of intense pressure without the buffers that sometimes help others succeed. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they wove together into a story of predicted struggle. This isn't the end of your journey; many have rewritten their stories by targeting those early grades or test scores with focused prep. You've got strengths we see, like your commitment to full-time study, and with targeted steps, you could shift this plot. Hang in there—law needs voices like yours.
